question: how make compost tea
title: How to Make Compost Tea: A Simple Guide That Actually Works
metaDescription: Compost tea is one of the most powerful things you can put on your plants. Scotty explains exactly how to make it at home — with just a bucket, an air pump, and good compost.
content:
I started making compost tea because I read about it in a book. I kept making it because I watched what it did to my plants. There is something deeply satisfying about taking a scoop of living compost, dropping it in a bucket of rainwater, bubbling it up for 24 hours, and then pouring that teeming microbial broth directly onto your soil. It's one of the simplest, most effective things you can do for plant health — and almost nobody talks about it.

Compost tea is made by steeping high-quality compost in clean water and aerating it with an aquarium air pump for 24 to 48 hours. The aeration feeds and multiplies the beneficial bacteria and fungi in the compost, creating a microbial-rich liquid that you apply directly to soil or as a foliar spray. It's inexpensive, genuinely effective, and something you can set up in your backyard with about ten dollars in hardware.

## What Compost Tea Actually Is and Why It Works

Before we get into the how, let me explain the why — because when you understand what's happening in that bucket, you'll never skip this step again.

Compost is alive. Every tablespoon of finished compost contains millions — sometimes billions — of bacteria, fungi, protozoa, and nematodes. That microbial community is what makes compost valuable. Not the NPK. The biology.

When you steep compost in aerated water, you're not extracting nutrients the way you'd make herbal tea. You're creating conditions for the existing microbial community to reproduce rapidly. Aeration — pumping oxygen through the water continuously — keeps conditions aerobic, which is the environment where beneficial bacteria and fungi thrive. Research published in PubMed shows that plate counts of bacteria and fungi in aerated compost teas are significantly higher than in the original compost after just 24 to 48 hours of brewing. You're making more microbes, not just moving the existing ones into solution.

When you apply that liquid to your soil, you're inoculating it with a fresh, diverse community of beneficial microorganisms. Those microbes colonize the rhizosphere — the zone around plant roots — and immediately go to work: breaking down organic matter, fixing nutrients into plant-available forms, producing compounds that stimulate root growth, and outcompeting pathogens for space and resources.

Research from PubMed on aerated compost tea confirms biofertilizer effects: soil restoration and plant health promotion, with growth promotion documented in lettuce, soybean, and sweet corn in organic cultivation conditions. This isn't folk knowledge. The science backs it up.

## What You Need to Make Compost Tea

The equipment list is short, cheap, and available at any hardware store or fish shop.

A clean bucket — five gallons is ideal. Clean is important here. Residue from chemical cleaners can harm the microbial community you're trying to build. Rinse a dedicated bucket thoroughly and use it only for compost tea.

An aquarium air pump with tubing and an airstone. You want something that produces vigorous bubbling — not just a slow trickle. The more surface area you're aerating, the better your microbial growth. A standard aquarium pump for a 20-40 gallon tank is usually sufficient for a five-gallon bucket.

Good compost — two to four scoops. This is where quality matters. The compost is your microbial seed stock. If your compost is mature, rich, and biologically active, your tea will be too. Remove any large sticks, leaves, or recognizable material before you add it. Worm castings make an exceptional compost tea base.

Clean water. Rainwater is ideal — no chlorine, no chloramine, no fluoride. If you're using municipal tap water, let it sit in an open bucket for several hours first so the chlorine can off-gas, or use an aquarium dechlorinator. Chlorine will slow down or kill the very microbes you're trying to grow.

Optionally: a catalyst. A small amount of unsulfured molasses — one tablespoon per five gallons — provides a simple sugar energy source for bacteria and dramatically speeds up microbial reproduction. Kelp meal or fish hydrolysate can add additional biology and micronutrients.

## The Brewing Process, Step by Step

This is as simple as it gets.

Fill your clean bucket with water. If using tap water, start the process the night before to allow it to sit.

Add your compost. Two to four generous scoops for a five-gallon bucket. Some people brew compost inside a mesh bag — like a large mesh paint strainer — to make cleanup easier. This works fine and produces equivalent results.

Add your molasses or other catalyst if using. One tablespoon of unsulfured molasses per five gallons.

Drop in your airstone, run the tubing to your pump, and start the aeration. You want vigorous, constant bubbling. The goal is oxygenated water throughout the entire volume — no dead zones where anaerobic conditions can develop.

Brew for 24 to 48 hours. I generally find 24 hours is excellent for bacterial-dominated teas, and 48 hours can encourage more fungal growth if your compost contains good fungal populations.

Apply the tea within four hours of finishing. Compost tea is alive — the microbial community needs to get into soil before the population crashes without the steady oxygen input it had in the bucket. Don't let it sit for days. Use it fresh.

A note from Rodale Institute's compost tea guide: use the tea as a soil drench around plants or as a foliar spray in early morning when leaf surfaces are slightly damp and temperatures are cooler. Both application methods have research support — soil drenching for rhizosphere inoculation, foliar application for disease suppression.

## What Compost Tea Does for Your Plants

The most immediate and dramatic effect is on plant vigor. Seedlings treated with aerated compost tea tend to establish faster, show stronger root development, and produce more robust early growth. A PubMed study on bacterial microbiota of aerated compost teas found significant effects on tomato growth. These aren't marginal improvements.

The second effect is disease suppression. Research published in PubMed found that diverse microbial communities in non-aerated compost teas suppress bacterial wilt — a devastating soil-borne pathogen. The mechanism is competitive exclusion: when you flood the rhizosphere with beneficial microbes, there simply isn't space or resources left for pathogens to establish. Another study showed that aerated compost tea produced with kelp and humic acid was the most consistent formulation for damping-off suppression in seedlings.

The third effect is soil biology improvement over time. Repeated compost tea applications build up the resident microbial population in your soil. You're not just treating your plants — you're rebuilding the ecosystem they grow in. Over a season of regular applications, the soil itself becomes more biologically active, more structurally stable, and more capable of supporting healthy plant growth without outside inputs.

This is what I mean when I say compost tea is about biology, not chemistry. You're not feeding plants directly with NPK. You're building the microbial infrastructure that makes nutrients available naturally.

## A Few Things I've Learned Along the Way

Quality of compost is everything. A mediocre pile makes mediocre tea. The richer and more biologically diverse your source compost, the better your tea. I use compost that's been managed hot — the thermophilic phase kills pathogens and weed seeds — and then fully cured. The biology in a mature, fully cured pile is different from a young pile, and it shows up in the tea.

Aerate aggressively. More air equals more aerobic bacteria. I've seen people use tiny aquarium pumps that barely move the water. Invest in something with real output. You want the compost material visibly circulating in the water column.

Brew in shade, not direct sun. Heat can depress dissolved oxygen and stress the microbial community. A cool, shaded location keeps the brew healthy.

Apply to moist soil. Dry soil is hostile to the incoming microbial community. Water your beds lightly before applying tea, and apply it in the cooler parts of the day — morning is ideal.

I don't worry about making this perfect. A simple, well-aerated 24-hour brew from good compost in clean water is going to do a lot of good in your garden. Don't let the details stop you from getting started.

Once you stop worrying about NPK fertilizers and start thinking about building microbial life in your soil, everything shifts. Compost tea is one of the most direct expressions of that shift — a jar of living biology, made for free, applied to the ground beneath your plants. Old as farming itself.
